learn.title: Iron-Air Batteries for Long Duration Storage
learn.intro: Iron-air batteries offer a breakthrough in long-duration energy storage, storing excess renewable energy for 100+ hours. This technology addresses grid instability by providing affordable, safe, and scalable solutions critical for deep decarbonization and 24/7 renewable energy reliability.
learn.c1_text: These batteries use iron and air: during discharge, iron oxidizes while drawing oxygen from the air. During charging, the process reverses. Like a giant alkaline battery, but scaled for grid-scale storage with 100+ hour duration, enabling seasonal energy storage.
learn.c2_text: Iron-air batteries use abundant materials (iron, air) and avoid rare earth metals. Costs are projected to drop below $100/kWh by 2030, making them 10x cheaper than lithium-ion for long-duration storage. Their non-flammable chemistry enhances safety.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 500 GW renewable energy by 2030, requiring massive storage. Iron-air batteries could store solar energy from summer monsoons for winter use. The National Energy Storage Mission prioritizes long-duration storage to stabilize grids with 500 GW renewable target.
learn.stat1: Iron-air batteries achieve 100+ hours of storage duration (US DOE)
learn.stat3: India's renewable energy target: 500 GW by 2030 (MNRE)
learn.stat4: Iron is 100x cheaper than lithium per kg (US DOE)

learn.title: Solar PV Panels and the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity using the photoelectric effect, a cornerstone of renewable energy. Understanding this process reveals how photovoltaic cells harness photons to generate clean power, driving India's 90 GW solar revolution and combating climate change.
learn.c1_text: When sunlight hits a solar cell, photons knock electrons loose from semiconductor materials (like silicon). This creates a flow of electrons—electricity. Think of it as a photon ‘ping-pong’ ball hitting electrons, freeing them to move and generate current.
learn.c2_text: PV cells contain p-n junctions: one side rich in electrons (n-type), another electron-poor (p-type). Photon impact creates electron-hole pairs. The junction’s electric field pushes electrons toward the front contact, forming a current when connected to a load. Efficiency depends on material quality and sunlight intensity.
learn.c3_text: India's solar capacity reached 90 GW in 2024, with rooftop solar incentivized under PM-KUSUM. Thejadau solar parks in Rajasthan and Gujarat exemplify large-scale deployment. The National Solar Mission targets 100 GW by 2027, leveraging photoelectric technology for rural electrification and industrial growth.
learn.stat1: India's solar installed capacity: 90 GW (2024, MNRE)
learn.stat3: India's rooftop solar target: 40 GW by 2024 (MNRE)

learn.title: Hydrogen Color Codes: Blue vs Green vs Grey
learn.intro: Hydrogen's color code reflects its production method and environmental impact. Green hydrogen (renewable-powered) is key to decarbonization, while grey (natural gas) dominates today. India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MT/year by 2030, reshaping energy and industry landscapes.
learn.c1_text: Green hydrogen is made via electrolysis powered by renewables (solar/wind), splitting water into H2 and O2. Like a water-splitting machine, it produces zero emissions. Cost is $3-6/kg today but expected to drop to $1-2/kg by 2030 with scaling.
learn.c2_text: Grey hydrogen uses natural gas without carbon capture (CCUS), emitting 9-12 tons CO2 per ton H2. Blue hydrogen captures 50-90% of emissions via CCUS, reducing but not eliminating carbon. Blue costs $1-4/kg, while grey is $1-2/kg but emits 95% more CO2 than green.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 MT/year production by 2030, with projects in Gujarat and Tamil Nadu. The mission targets $2-3/kg green H2 by 2030, supporting 200 GW renewable energy and decarbonizing steel, ammonia, and transport sectors.
learn.stat1: India's green hydrogen target: 5 MT/year by 2030 (IOG)
learn.stat2: Grey hydrogen emits 9-12 tons CO2 per ton H2 (IEA)

learn.title: Green Steel Production with Hydrogen
learn.intro: Green steel uses hydrogen instead of coal in blast furnaces, cutting CO2 emissions by 95%. With steel production responsible for 7-9% of global emissions, India’s Green Steel Mission aims for 25% low-carbon steel by 2030, transforming heavy industry through hydrogen-based direct reduction.
learn.c1_text: Hydrogen reduces iron ore in blast furnaces instead of coal, producing pure H2O as a byproduct instead of CO2. Like a coal-free furnace, it uses H2 to strip oxygen from iron, creating sponge iron. This process emits 95% less CO2 than traditional methods.
learn.c2_text: Hydrogen-based Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) plants use 30-40% less energy than coal-based methods. Emissions drop to 0.1-0.2 tons CO2/ton steel vs. 1.8-2.0 tons for conventional steel. The H2DRI process, pioneered in Sweden, scales globally with green hydrogen availability.
learn.c3_text: India’s Green Steel Mission targets 25% low-carbon steel by 2030. Projects like Tata Steel’s H2DRI in Jamshedpur use 100% green hydrogen. The National Green Hydrogen Mission supports electrolyzer supply chains, while subsidies under PLI schemes accelerate adoption in sectors like automotive and infrastructure.
learn.stat1: Green steel emits 95% less CO2 than conventional steel (IEA)

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage as Grid Battery
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage (PHS) is the world’s largest battery, storing energy by pumping water uphill when excess power is available. With 175 GW global capacity, it stabilizes grids with renewables, supporting India’s 500 GW target through scalable, low-cost, and long-duration storage solutions.
learn.c1_text: During surplus energy, pumps push water to an upper reservoir. When demand rises, water flows downhill through turbines to generate electricity. Like a gravitational battery, it stores 5-25 hours of energy and scales to gigawatt levels, with 70-85% round-trip efficiency.
learn.c2_text: PHS provides rapid response (seconds), frequency regulation, and baseload capacity. It’s 2-3x cheaper than lithium-ion for long-duration storage. Modern plants use variable-speed pump-turbines for better efficiency and can integrate with solar/wind farms for seamless energy shifting.
learn.c3_text: India’s PHS capacity is 7.6 GW (2024), with projects like Nathpa Jhakri (1.5 GW) in Himachal Pradesh. The National Hydroelectric Power Corporation plans 20 GW by 2030. New 10 GW+ projects in Uttarakhand and Sikkim leverage mountainous terrain for cost-effective renewable integration.

learn.title: Floating Offshore Wind Platforms
learn.intro: Floating offshore wind platforms unlock deep-water wind resources, expanding viable sites beyond 50m depth. With 30 GW global capacity by 2030, they complement fixed-bottom turbines, enabling India’s 60 GW offshore wind target through innovative technology and cost reductions.
learn.c1_text: Floating platforms use mooring systems and cables to connect turbines to shore. Unlike fixed-bottom, they work in 100-3,000m depths. Like underwater kites, they harness stronger, steadier winds. Spar, semi-submersible, and TLP designs offer stability in varying ocean conditions.
learn.c2_text: Floating wind costs dropped 60% since 2012, reaching $5,000/kW in 2024. They avoid seabed disruption and access untapped resources. Projects like Hywind Scotland (50 MW) prove viability, with 20% higher capacity factors than land-based wind due to consistent deep-sea winds.
learn.c3_text: India’s 60 GW offshore wind target by 2030 includes floating projects in 100-200m depths. Kerala and Tamil Nadu are exploring pilot projects under the National Offshore Wind Energy Policy. The 1 GW Kanyakumari floating wind farm, planned by 2030, will set a precedent for deep-water renewable energy.
learn.stat1: India's offshore wind target: 60 GW by 2030 (MNRE)
learn.stat2: Floating wind cost reduction: 60% since 2012 (US DOE)
learn.stat3: Hywind Scotland capacity: 50 MW (2017, US DOE)
